OU · BME MATHS · TUTORIAL
Worked tutorial · a companion to the syllabus & study plan

Learn the Maths, unit by unit

Every unit across the four papers, taught the way you'd actually study it: the core idea in plain words, the key results you must memorise, a fully worked example, and the mistakes that quietly cost marks.

◆ The idea ◆ Key results ◆ Worked example ◆ Watch out
BS 101 MT · Semester 1

Mathematics – I

The calculus toolkit. You'll decide whether infinite sums settle down, sketch curves from their behaviour, extend differentiation to surfaces, and turn integration into a way of measuring area, volume and flow.

Unit I

Sequences & Series

A series is an infinite sum \(a_1+a_2+a_3+\cdots\). The only question that matters is: does it add up to a finite number (converges) or run off to infinity (diverges)? You never sum it by hand — you apply a test. Learning which test to reach for is the whole game.

Key results — the convergence tests

p-series: \(\sum \frac{1}{n^p}\) converges iff \(p>1\).

D'Alembert's ratio test: let \(L=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right|\). Then \(L<1\Rightarrow\) converges, \(L>1\Rightarrow\) diverges, \(L=1\Rightarrow\) inconclusive.

Cauchy's root test: \(L=\lim (a_n)^{1/n}\); same rule as the ratio test.

Raabe's test (use when \(L=1\)): \(\lim n\!\left(\frac{a_n}{a_{n+1}}-1\right)>1\Rightarrow\) converges.

Leibnitz (alternating): \(\sum(-1)^{n}a_n\) converges if \(a_n\) decreases and \(a_n\to0\).

Absolute convergence: if \(\sum|a_n|\) converges, so does \(\sum a_n\).

Worked example

Test \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n}{2^{n}}\) for convergence.

Choose the test Terms have a power \(2^n\) in the denominator, so the ratio test is natural.

Form the ratio\[\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}=\frac{(n+1)/2^{\,n+1}}{n/2^{\,n}}=\frac{n+1}{2n}\]

Take the limit\[L=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n+1}{2n}=\frac{1}{2}\]

Conclude Since \(L=\tfrac12<1\), the series converges.

Watch out

  • When the ratio test gives \(L=1\) it tells you nothing — switch to Raabe's test, don't guess.
  • \(a_n\to0\) does not prove convergence (the harmonic series \(\sum 1/n\) diverges). It's only a necessary condition.
  • For alternating series, check both Leibnitz conditions — decreasing and tending to zero.
Exam focus: nearly every paper has a "test for convergence" question. Practise spotting the right test in under ten seconds.
Unit II

Differential Calculus

This unit connects a function's derivative to its shape. The mean value theorems guarantee a point where the tangent matches the average slope; curvature measures how sharply a curve bends; Taylor's series rebuilds a function from its derivatives at one point.

Key results

Rolle's theorem: if \(f(a)=f(b)\) then \(f'(c)=0\) for some \(c\in(a,b)\).

Lagrange's mean value theorem: \(\displaystyle f'(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}\).

Taylor's series: \(\displaystyle f(x)=f(a)+f'(a)(x-a)+\frac{f''(a)}{2!}(x-a)^2+\cdots\)

Radius of curvature: \(\displaystyle \rho=\frac{\left(1+y'^{\,2}\right)^{3/2}}{|y''|}\).

Worked example

Find the radius of curvature of \(y=x^{2}\) at the origin.

Derivatives \(y'=2x,\quad y''=2\).

Evaluate at (0,0) \(y'=0,\ y''=2\).

Apply the formula\[\rho=\frac{(1+0)^{3/2}}{|2|}=\frac{1}{2}\]

So the parabola bends as tightly as a circle of radius \(\tfrac12\) at its vertex.

Watch out

  • Rolle's theorem needs \(f(a)=f(b)\) and continuity/differentiability — state all hypotheses or lose marks.
  • In the curvature formula the numerator power is \(3/2\), not \(1/2\). This is the single most common slip.
Exam focus: radius/centre of curvature and Taylor expansions are recurring numerical questions — memorise the formulas cold.
Unit III

Functions of Several Real Variables

Now the input is a point \((x,y)\), not a single number. Partial derivatives measure change in one direction at a time; the Jacobian tracks how areas stretch under a change of variables; and finding maxima/minima needs a second-derivative test built for surfaces.

Key results

Jacobian: \(\displaystyle \frac{\partial(u,v)}{\partial(x,y)}=\begin{vmatrix}u_x & u_y\\ v_x & v_y\end{vmatrix}\).

Maxima/minima test at a stationary point \((f_x=f_y=0)\). Let \(r=f_{xx},\ s=f_{xy},\ t=f_{yy}\):

  • \(rt-s^{2}>0\) and \(r<0\) → maximum
  • \(rt-s^{2}>0\) and \(r>0\) → minimum
  • \(rt-s^{2}<0\) → saddle point (no extremum)

Lagrange multipliers (constrained extrema of \(f\) subject to \(g=0\)): solve \(\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g\) together with \(g=0\).

Worked example

Find and classify the stationary point of \(f=x^{2}+xy+y^{2}-3x\).

Stationary point \(f_x=2x+y-3=0,\quad f_y=x+2y=0\). Solving: \(x=2,\ y=-1\).

Second derivatives \(r=f_{xx}=2,\ s=f_{xy}=1,\ t=f_{yy}=2\).

Test \(rt-s^{2}=4-1=3>0\) and \(r=2>0\).

Conclude A minimum at \((2,-1)\).

Watch out

  • \(rt-s^2=0\) is inconclusive — the test fails and you'd need another method. Note it, don't force an answer.
  • In Lagrange problems, don't forget the constraint equation itself — it's the extra equation you need.
Exam focus: constrained maxima/minima via Lagrange multipliers is a classic 10-mark question.
Unit IV

Multiple Integrals

A double integral \(\iint f\,dA\) adds up a quantity over a 2-D region; a triple integral does it over a solid. The two skills examiners test: changing the order of integration (redrawing the region and re-reading its limits) and using integrals to find areas and volumes.

Key results

Area of a region \(R\): \(\displaystyle A=\iint_R dx\,dy\).

Volume under \(z=f(x,y)\): \(\displaystyle V=\iint_R f(x,y)\,dx\,dy\).

Polar form (for circular regions): \(dx\,dy \to r\,dr\,d\theta\).

Change of order: sketch the region, then re-express the limits with the outer/inner variables swapped — the region is the same, only the description changes.

Worked example

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}\!\int_{0}^{x} (x+y)\,dy\,dx\).

Inner integral (over y)\[\int_0^x (x+y)\,dy=\Big[xy+\tfrac{y^2}{2}\Big]_0^x=x^2+\tfrac{x^2}{2}=\tfrac{3x^2}{2}\]

Outer integral (over x)\[\int_0^1 \tfrac{3x^2}{2}\,dx=\tfrac{3}{2}\cdot\tfrac{x^3}{3}\Big|_0^1=\tfrac{1}{2}\]

Value \(=\tfrac12\).

Watch out

  • Integrate the inner variable first, treating the outer variable as a constant.
  • When changing order you must sketch the region — guessing the new limits is where most marks are lost.
  • Switching to polar? Remember the extra factor of \(r\) in \(r\,dr\,d\theta\).
Exam focus: "change the order of integration and evaluate" appears almost every year.
Unit V

Vector Calculus

Three operators unlock this unit: gradient (steepest increase of a scalar), divergence (how much a field spreads out), and curl (how much it rotates). Three big theorems — Green's, Gauss's and Stoke's — then let you swap a hard integral for an easier one.

Key results

Gradient: \(\nabla\varphi=\varphi_x\,\mathbf i+\varphi_y\,\mathbf j+\varphi_z\,\mathbf k\).

Divergence: \(\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf F=\dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial x}+\dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial y}+\dfrac{\partial F_3}{\partial z}\) (a scalar).

Curl: \(\nabla\times\mathbf F\) (a vector, from the determinant of \(\mathbf i,\mathbf j,\mathbf k\), \(\partial\), and \(\mathbf F\)).

Green's theorem: \(\displaystyle\oint_C (P\,dx+Q\,dy)=\iint_R\!\left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x}-\frac{\partial P}{\partial y}\right)dx\,dy\).

Gauss divergence: \(\displaystyle\iint_S \mathbf F\!\cdot\!\mathbf n\,dS=\iiint_V (\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf F)\,dV\).

Stoke's theorem: \(\displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf F\!\cdot\!d\mathbf r=\iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf F)\!\cdot\!\mathbf n\,dS\).

Worked example

Find \(\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf F\) and \(\nabla\times\mathbf F\) for \(\mathbf F=(x^{2}y)\,\mathbf i+(yz)\,\mathbf j+(xz)\,\mathbf k\).

Divergence\[\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf F=\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2y)+\frac{\partial}{\partial y}(yz)+\frac{\partial}{\partial z}(xz)=2xy+z+x\]

Curl Component-wise, \(\nabla\times\mathbf F=(0-y)\,\mathbf i-(z-0)\,\mathbf j+(0-x^2)\,\mathbf k\)\[=-y\,\mathbf i-z\,\mathbf j-x^{2}\,\mathbf k\]

Watch out

  • Divergence is a scalar; curl is a vector. Mixing them up is an instant giveaway.
  • Mind the middle sign in the curl determinant expansion — the \(\mathbf j\) term carries a minus.
  • Green's theorem is the 2-D special case of Stoke's — knowing that saves memorising two things.
Exam focus: "verify Green's / Gauss's / Stoke's theorem" for a given field and region is a guaranteed long-answer question.
BS 201 MT · Semester 2

Mathematics – II

Linear algebra and differential equations — the two engines of applied maths. You'll solve linear systems and eigenvalue problems, then learn a ladder of techniques for differential equations, from first-order tricks to series solutions and named special functions.

Unit I

Matrices

Matrices package linear systems compactly. Rank tells you how many independent equations you really have; eigenvalues and eigenvectors reveal the directions a matrix merely stretches; and Cayley–Hamilton says every matrix obeys its own characteristic equation.

Key results

Eigenvalues solve the characteristic equation \(\det(A-\lambda I)=0\).

Eigenvector for \(\lambda\): any non-zero \(\mathbf x\) with \((A-\lambda I)\mathbf x=\mathbf 0\).

Trace & product checks: sum of eigenvalues \(=\) trace of \(A\); product \(=\det A\).

Cayley–Hamilton: \(A\) satisfies its own characteristic polynomial — a fast route to \(A^{-1}\) and high powers of \(A\).

Worked example

Find the eigenvalues of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}4&1\\2&3\end{pmatrix}\).

Characteristic equation\[\det(A-\lambda I)=\begin{vmatrix}4-\lambda&1\\2&3-\lambda\end{vmatrix}=(4-\lambda)(3-\lambda)-2=0\]

Expand\[\lambda^{2}-7\lambda+10=0\]

Solve \((\lambda-2)(\lambda-5)=0\Rightarrow \lambda=2,\ 5\).

Sanity check Sum \(=7=\) trace ✓, product \(=10=\det A\) ✓.

Watch out

  • Reducing a matrix to echelon form: apply row operations consistently — a single arithmetic slip corrupts the rank.
  • Use the trace and determinant checks on your eigenvalues; they catch most errors instantly.
  • For a quadratic form, reduction to canonical form must be by an orthogonal transformation.
Exam focus: verifying Cayley–Hamilton and using it to find \(A^{-1}\) is a repeat favourite.
Unit II

Ordinary Differential Equations — First Order

A first-order ODE relates \(y\) and \(\tfrac{dy}{dx}\). Success is all about recognising the type: is it exact, linear, Bernoulli, Clairaut? Each type has a fixed recipe, so classification is 80% of the work.

Key results

Exact \(M\,dx+N\,dy=0\): exact iff \(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}=\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\).

Linear \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}+Py=Q\): integrating factor \(\mu=e^{\int P\,dx}\), then \((\mu y)'=\mu Q\).

Bernoulli \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}+Py=Qy^{n}\): substitute \(v=y^{1-n}\) to linearise.

Clairaut \(y=px+f(p)\) where \(p=y'\): general solution \(y=cx+f(c)\).

Worked example

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}+y=e^{x}\).

Identify Linear with \(P=1,\ Q=e^x\).

Integrating factor \(\mu=e^{\int 1\,dx}=e^{x}\).

Multiply & recognise\[\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(y\,e^{x}\right)=e^{x}\cdot e^{x}=e^{2x}\]

Integrate\[y\,e^{x}=\frac{e^{2x}}{2}+C\ \Rightarrow\ y=\frac{e^{x}}{2}+Ce^{-x}\]

Watch out

  • Get the equation into standard form \(y'+Py=Q\) before reading off \(P\) — a stray coefficient ruins \(\mu\).
  • Bernoulli only linearises after the substitution — don't try the integrating factor directly.
  • Add the constant of integration; a "particular vs general solution" mark often hangs on it.
Exam focus: orthogonal trajectories build directly on the linear/exact methods — expect them.
Unit III

Linear DEs of Higher Order

Second- and higher-order linear equations split into two jobs: the complementary function (CF) from the roots of the auxiliary equation, and the particular integral (PI) driven by the right-hand side. The full solution is CF + PI.

Key results

Auxiliary equation for constant coefficients: replace \(\tfrac{d}{dx}\) with \(m\) and solve for the roots.

  • Real distinct \(m_1,m_2\): \(\;y_c=c_1e^{m_1x}+c_2e^{m_2x}\)
  • Repeated root \(m\): \(\;y_c=(c_1+c_2x)e^{mx}\)
  • Complex \(\alpha\pm i\beta\): \(\;y_c=e^{\alpha x}(c_1\cos\beta x+c_2\sin\beta x)\)

Variation of parameters handles a general right-hand side when the operator method is awkward.

Euler–Cauchy \(x^2y''+axy'+by=0\): substitute \(x=e^{t}\) to get constant coefficients.

Worked example

Solve \(y''-5y'+6y=0\).

Auxiliary equation \(m^{2}-5m+6=0\).

Roots \((m-2)(m-3)=0\Rightarrow m=2,\ 3\) (real, distinct).

General solution\[y=c_1e^{2x}+c_2e^{3x}\]

Watch out

  • For a repeated root, the extra factor of \(x\) is essential — \(c_1e^{mx}+c_2e^{mx}\) is really just one constant.
  • The PI must be found separately and added; the CF alone is not the answer to a non-homogeneous equation.
  • When the forcing term duplicates a CF term, multiply your trial PI by \(x\) to avoid a clash.
Exam focus: a full CF + PI solve, plus one variation-of-parameters problem, is standard.
Unit IV

Series Solutions & Special Integrals

When coefficients aren't constant, assume the solution is a power series and grind out its coefficients. Near a "regular singular point" you use the Frobenius method. This unit also introduces the Beta and Gamma functions — compact ways to write otherwise nasty integrals.

Key results

Gamma function: \(\Gamma(n+1)=n\,\Gamma(n)\), \(\Gamma(n+1)=n!\) for integers, and \(\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac12\right)=\sqrt{\pi}\).

Beta function: \(\displaystyle B(m,n)=\int_0^1 x^{m-1}(1-x)^{n-1}dx=\frac{\Gamma(m)\,\Gamma(n)}{\Gamma(m+n)}\).

Frobenius: assume \(y=\sum_{k\ge 0} a_k x^{k+r}\); the indicial equation fixes \(r\), and a recurrence fixes the \(a_k\).

Worked example

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x^{3}(1-x)^{2}\,dx\) using the Beta function.

Match the form This is \(B(4,3)\) since \(m-1=3\Rightarrow m=4\) and \(n-1=2\Rightarrow n=3\).

Apply the identity\[B(4,3)=\frac{\Gamma(4)\,\Gamma(3)}{\Gamma(7)}=\frac{3!\,\cdot 2!}{6!}=\frac{6\cdot 2}{720}=\frac{1}{60}\]

Watch out

  • Beta's exponents are \(m-1\) and \(n-1\) — subtract one before reading off \(m,n\).
  • The indicial equation comes from the lowest power of \(x\); don't skip it.
Exam focus: Beta–Gamma evaluation of definite integrals is a reliable, quick-scoring question.
Unit V

Special Functions — Legendre & Bessel

Two named families that fall out of physics problems in spherical and cylindrical geometry. Legendre polynomials \(P_n(x)\) and Bessel functions \(J_n(x)\) come with generating functions, recurrence relations and orthogonality — properties you use rather than derive.

Key results

Rodrigue's formula: \(\displaystyle P_n(x)=\frac{1}{2^{n}\,n!}\frac{d^{n}}{dx^{n}}\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{n}\).

First few: \(P_0=1,\ P_1=x,\ P_2=\tfrac12(3x^2-1)\).

Orthogonality: \(\displaystyle\int_{-1}^{1}P_m(x)P_n(x)\,dx=0\) for \(m\neq n\).

Bessel recurrence: \(\displaystyle J_{n-1}(x)+J_{n+1}(x)=\frac{2n}{x}J_n(x)\).

Worked example

Derive \(P_2(x)\) from Rodrigue's formula.

Set up \(n=2\): \(P_2=\dfrac{1}{2^{2}\,2!}\dfrac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}(x^{2}-1)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{8}\dfrac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}(x^{4}-2x^{2}+1)\).

Differentiate twice \(\dfrac{d}{dx}=4x^{3}-4x\), then \(\dfrac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}=12x^{2}-4\).

Simplify\[P_2=\frac{12x^{2}-4}{8}=\frac{3x^{2}-1}{2}\]

Watch out

  • Keep the \(\tfrac{1}{2^n n!}\) prefactor — dropping it gives the right shape but the wrong polynomial.
  • Orthogonality of \(P_n\) is over \([-1,1]\) specifically; the interval matters.
Exam focus: proving a recurrence relation or the orthogonality property is a standard theory question.
BS 901 MT · Semester 3 · BME-specific

Mathematics – III

The mathematics behind biosignals. Complex analysis gives elegant ways to evaluate real integrals; Fourier series decomposes any periodic signal into sine waves; and partial differential equations model heat, waves and diffusion in the body.

Unit I

Functions of Complex Variables

A complex function \(f(z)=u+iv\) is analytic when it's differentiable in a whole region. The test is the Cauchy–Riemann equations. Analytic functions are astonishingly rigid — knowing them on a boundary determines them inside, which is what Cauchy's theorems capture.

Key results

Cauchy–Riemann equations: \(f=u+iv\) is analytic iff \(\;u_x=v_y\;\) and \(\;u_y=-v_x\).

Harmonic: \(u\) and \(v\) each satisfy Laplace's equation \(u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0\).

Cauchy's integral theorem: \(\displaystyle\oint_C f(z)\,dz=0\) if \(f\) is analytic inside and on \(C\).

Cauchy's integral formula: \(\displaystyle f(a)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C\frac{f(z)}{z-a}\,dz\).

Worked example

Show \(f(z)=z^{2}\) is analytic and find its derivative via C–R.

Separate parts \(z^2=(x+iy)^2=(x^2-y^2)+i(2xy)\), so \(u=x^2-y^2,\ v=2xy\).

Check C–R \(u_x=2x=v_y\ \checkmark\) and \(u_y=-2y=-v_x\ \checkmark\).

Conclude Analytic everywhere, with \(f'(z)=u_x+iv_x=2x+i\,2y=2z\), as expected.

Watch out

  • C–R being satisfied at a point isn't enough for analyticity — the partials must also be continuous in a neighbourhood.
  • Watch the sign: the second C–R equation is \(u_y=-v_x\), not \(+\).
Exam focus: "find the harmonic conjugate" and "evaluate using Cauchy's integral formula" are staples.
Unit II

Residue Calculus

The residue theorem is complex analysis' superpower: a contour integral equals \(2\pi i\) times the sum of residues inside. That turns hard real integrals into a bookkeeping exercise over poles.

Key results

Residue at a simple pole \(z=a\): \(\displaystyle \operatorname{Res}_{z=a}f=\lim_{z\to a}(z-a)f(z)\).

Residue theorem: \(\displaystyle\oint_C f(z)\,dz=2\pi i\sum(\text{residues inside }C)\).

Laurent series classifies singularities: a finite principal part ⇒ pole; infinite ⇒ essential singularity.

Worked example

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{z}{(z-1)(z-3)}\,dz\).

Locate poles At \(z=1\) and \(z=3\). Only \(z=1\) lies inside \(|z|=2\).

Residue at z = 1\[\lim_{z\to1}(z-1)\frac{z}{(z-1)(z-3)}=\frac{1}{1-3}=-\frac12\]

Apply the theorem\[\oint=2\pi i\left(-\tfrac12\right)=-\pi i\]

Watch out

  • Only count poles inside the contour — check each against the given circle's radius.
  • For a pole of order \(m>1\), use the derivative formula, not the simple-pole limit.
Exam focus: evaluating a real integral \(\int_0^{2\pi}\) or \(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\) by residues is a high-mark question.
Unit III

Fourier Series

Any reasonable periodic function is a sum of sines and cosines. The Fourier coefficients pick out "how much" of each frequency is present — the exact idea behind analysing an ECG or EEG trace into its rhythms.

Key results

Series on \((-\pi,\pi)\): \(\displaystyle f(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\big(a_n\cos nx+b_n\sin nx\big)\).

Coefficients: \(\displaystyle a_n=\frac1\pi\!\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\! f\cos nx\,dx,\quad b_n=\frac1\pi\!\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\! f\sin nx\,dx\).

Symmetry shortcut: even \(f\) → cosine terms only \((b_n=0)\); odd \(f\) → sine terms only \((a_n=0)\).

Worked example

Find the Fourier series of \(f(x)=x\) on \((-\pi,\pi)\).

Use symmetry \(x\) is odd, so \(a_0=a_n=0\) — only sine terms survive.

Sine coefficient\[b_n=\frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi}x\sin nx\,dx=\frac{2}{\pi}\left[-\frac{x\cos nx}{n}+\frac{\sin nx}{n^2}\right]_0^{\pi}\]

Evaluate \(=\dfrac{2}{\pi}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{\pi\cos n\pi}{n}\right)=-\dfrac{2\cos n\pi}{n}=\dfrac{2(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\).

Result\[x=2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\sin nx\]

Watch out

  • Always test even/odd symmetry first — it can halve the work by killing a whole family of coefficients.
  • Integration by parts on \(x\sin nx\) is where sign errors creep in; keep the bookkeeping tidy.
  • Half-range series require you to extend the function (as even or odd) before computing.
Exam focus: expand a given \(f(x)\) as a Fourier series, then use it to sum a numerical series — a two-part classic.
Unit IV

Partial Differential Equations

A PDE involves partial derivatives of an unknown function of several variables. First you form the PDE by eliminating arbitrary constants or functions; then you solve it — Lagrange's method for linear first-order, Charpit's for non-linear.

Key results

Notation: \(p=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x},\ q=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial y}\).

Lagrange's linear equation \(Pp+Qq=R\): solve the auxiliary system \(\displaystyle\frac{dx}{P}=\frac{dy}{Q}=\frac{dz}{R}\), giving two independent solutions \(u=c_1,\ v=c_2\); general solution \(\phi(u,v)=0\).

Charpit's method handles general non-linear first-order equations \(f(x,y,z,p,q)=0\).

Worked example

Solve \(y\,p - x\,q = 0\) by Lagrange's method.

Auxiliary equations\[\frac{dx}{y}=\frac{dy}{-x}=\frac{dz}{0}\]

From the last ratio \(dz=0\Rightarrow z=c_1\).

From the first pair \(x\,dx+y\,dy=0\Rightarrow x^2+y^2=c_2\).

General solution \(\phi(z,\ x^2+y^2)=0\), i.e. \(z=F(x^2+y^2)\).

Watch out

  • Match \(P,Q,R\) carefully to \(\tfrac{dx}{P}=\tfrac{dy}{Q}=\tfrac{dz}{R}\); a swapped term derails everything.
  • When forming a PDE, count the arbitrary constants — you need that many differentiations to eliminate them.
Exam focus: one "form the PDE" and one "solve by Lagrange/Charpit" question is the usual split.
Unit V

Fourier Series Applied to PDEs

The payoff unit: separation of variables reduces a PDE to ordinary ODEs, and Fourier series stitches the pieces back into a solution that fits the boundary conditions. This is exactly how the heat, wave and Laplace equations are solved.

Key results

Heat equation: \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}=c^{2}\dfrac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}}\) (diffusion).

Wave equation: \(\dfrac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial t^{2}}=c^{2}\dfrac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}}\) (vibration).

Laplace's equation: \(\dfrac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}}+\dfrac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial y^{2}}=0\) (steady state).

Method: assume \(u(x,t)=X(x)\,T(t)\), separate, solve each ODE, then match boundary/initial conditions with a Fourier series.

Worked example — the method in outline

Solve the heat equation \(u_t=c^2u_{xx}\) with \(u=0\) at \(x=0,L\).

Separate Put \(u=X(x)T(t)\). Dividing, \(\dfrac{T'}{c^2T}=\dfrac{X''}{X}=-\lambda\) (a constant).

Solve the space part \(X''+\lambda X=0\) with \(X(0)=X(L)=0\) gives \(X_n=\sin\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\), \(\lambda_n=\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2\).

Solve the time part \(T_n=e^{-c^2\lambda_n t}\).

Superpose\[u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-c^2\lambda_n t}\]with \(b_n\) fixed by the initial temperature via a Fourier sine series.

Watch out

  • The sign of the separation constant \(-\lambda\) matters — the wrong choice gives exponentials where you need sines.
  • Apply the boundary conditions before the initial condition; boundaries fix the eigenfunctions, the initial condition fixes the coefficients.
Exam focus: a full separation-of-variables solve of the 1-D heat or wave equation is the big question of this paper.
BS 401 MT · Semester 4

Mathematics – IV

Transforms and computation — the working mathematics of engineering. Laplace and Fourier transforms turn calculus into algebra; Z-transforms do the same for discrete signals; numerical methods deliver answers when formulas run out; and regression fits models to real data.

Unit I

Laplace Transforms

The Laplace transform \(\mathcal L\{f(t)\}=F(s)\) converts a differential equation in \(t\) into an algebraic equation in \(s\). Solve the algebra, then invert. It's the fastest route through initial-value problems.

Key results — the transform table

\(\mathcal L\{1\}=\dfrac1s\)

\(\mathcal L\{t^n\}=\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}\)

\(\mathcal L\{e^{at}\}=\dfrac{1}{s-a}\)

\(\mathcal L\{\sin at\}=\dfrac{a}{s^2+a^2}\)

\(\mathcal L\{\cos at\}=\dfrac{s}{s^2+a^2}\)

\(\mathcal L\{f'(t)\}=sF(s)-f(0)\)

First shifting: \(\mathcal L\{e^{at}f(t)\}=F(s-a)\).   Convolution: \(\mathcal L\{f*g\}=F(s)\,G(s)\).

Worked example

Solve \(y'+y=1\), \(y(0)=0\), using Laplace transforms.

Transform both sides \(sY(s)-y(0)+Y(s)=\dfrac1s\), and \(y(0)=0\), so \((s+1)Y=\dfrac1s\).

Solve algebraically\[Y(s)=\frac{1}{s(s+1)}=\frac{1}{s}-\frac{1}{s+1}\quad\text{(partial fractions)}\]

Invert\[y(t)=1-e^{-t}\]

Watch out

  • \(\mathcal L\{f'\}=sF(s)-f(0)\) — forgetting the \(-f(0)\) term drops the initial condition entirely.
  • Partial fractions before inverting; the table only recognises standard shapes.
  • The shifting theorems save enormous effort — learn to spot the \(e^{at}\) factor.
Exam focus: solving an IVP by Laplace transform, and an inverse transform by convolution, are both near-certain.
Unit II

Fourier Transforms

Where the Fourier series handles periodic signals, the Fourier transform handles one-off (non-periodic) signals over the whole line — the mathematical basis of frequency analysis and filtering in signal processing.

Key results

Fourier transform: \(\displaystyle F(s)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(x)\,e^{isx}\,dx\).

Fourier sine transform: \(\displaystyle F_s(s)=\int_0^{\infty}f(x)\sin sx\,dx\).

Fourier cosine transform: \(\displaystyle F_c(s)=\int_0^{\infty}f(x)\cos sx\,dx\).

Even functions pair naturally with the cosine transform; odd functions with the sine transform.

Worked example

Find the Fourier cosine transform of \(f(x)=e^{-ax}\ (a>0)\).

Set up\[F_c(s)=\int_0^{\infty}e^{-ax}\cos sx\,dx\]

Standard integral\[\int_0^{\infty}e^{-ax}\cos sx\,dx=\frac{a}{a^{2}+s^{2}}\]

So \(F_c(s)=\dfrac{a}{a^{2}+s^{2}}\).

Watch out

  • Constant conventions (the \(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\) factor) vary between textbooks — follow the one your course prescribes.
  • Choose sine vs cosine transform based on the boundary condition given, not at random.
Exam focus: finding the transform of \(e^{-ax}\) or a rectangular pulse, and applying the convolution theorem.
Unit III

Z-Transforms

The Z-transform is the Laplace transform's discrete cousin. It converts a difference equation (a recurrence in a sequence \(y_n\)) into algebra in \(z\) — the standard tool for digital filters and sampled-data systems.

Key results

\(Z\{1\}=\dfrac{z}{z-1}\)

\(Z\{a^n\}=\dfrac{z}{z-a}\)

\(Z\{n\}=\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}\)

Shifting: \(Z\{y_{n+1}\}=zY(z)-z\,y_0\)

Worked example

Solve \(y_{n+1}-2y_n=0\), \(y_0=3\), using Z-transforms.

Transform \(zY(z)-z\,y_0-2Y(z)=0\Rightarrow (z-2)Y=3z\).

Solve \(Y(z)=\dfrac{3z}{z-2}\).

Invert Comparing with \(Z\{a^n\}=\dfrac{z}{z-a}\) gives \(a=2\), so \(y_n=3\cdot 2^{n}\).

Watch out

  • The shift rule brings in the initial term \(y_0\) — the discrete analogue of the \(-f(0)\) in Laplace.
  • Keep the \(z\) in the numerator when inverting; \(\tfrac{z}{z-a}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{z-a}\) invert differently.
Exam focus: solving a second-order difference equation by Z-transform is the signature question.
Unit IV

Numerical Methods

When an equation has no clean formula, you compute an answer by iteration. This unit is a toolbox: root-finding, solving linear systems, interpolation from a table, and stepping an ODE forward in time.

Key results

Newton–Raphson (roots): \(\displaystyle x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\).

Gauss–Seidel (linear systems): iterate each variable using the newest available values.

Newton's forward interpolation: \(\displaystyle y=y_0+p\,\Delta y_0+\frac{p(p-1)}{2!}\Delta^2y_0+\cdots,\ \ p=\frac{x-x_0}{h}\).

Runge–Kutta 4th order (ODEs): a weighted average of four slopes per step — the workhorse method.

Worked example

Find \(\sqrt{2}\) by Newton–Raphson using \(f(x)=x^{2}-2\), starting at \(x_0=1.5\).

Iteration formula \(f'(x)=2x\), so \(x_{n+1}=x_n-\dfrac{x_n^{2}-2}{2x_n}=\dfrac{x_n}{2}+\dfrac{1}{x_n}\).

Step 1 \(x_1=\dfrac{1.5}{2}+\dfrac{1}{1.5}=0.75+0.6667=1.4167\).

Step 2 \(x_2=\dfrac{1.4167}{2}+\dfrac{1}{1.4167}=0.7083+0.7059=1.4142\).

Converged to \(\sqrt2\approx1.4142\) in two steps.

Watch out

  • Newton–Raphson can diverge from a poor start or where \(f'(x)\approx0\) — pick a sensible \(x_0\).
  • Gauss–Seidel converges reliably only when the system is diagonally dominant; rearrange rows if needed.
  • Use forward differences near the start of a table, backward near the end.
Exam focus: two Newton–Raphson iterations, a Gauss–Seidel round, and one RK4 step are common short-to-medium questions.
Unit V

Curve Fitting, Correlation & Regression

Given scattered data, least squares finds the best-fitting line; correlation measures how tightly two variables move together; regression gives the equation to predict one from the other. This is the statistics you'll actually use analysing experimental results.

Key results

Least-squares line \(y=a+bx\) via the normal equations:

\[\sum y=na+b\sum x,\qquad \sum xy=a\sum x+b\sum x^{2}\]

Karl Pearson's correlation coefficient: \(\displaystyle r=\frac{\sum(x-\bar x)(y-\bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum(x-\bar x)^2\,\sum(y-\bar y)^2}}\), with \(-1\le r\le 1\).

Spearman's rank correlation: \(\displaystyle \rho=1-\frac{6\sum d^{2}}{n(n^{2}-1)}\), where \(d\) is the difference in ranks.

Worked example

Interpret correlation for four data points where \(\sum d^2 = 2\), \(n=4\) (ranked data).

Apply Spearman\[\rho=1-\frac{6(2)}{4(16-1)}=1-\frac{12}{60}=1-0.2=0.8\]

Interpret \(\rho=0.8\) indicates a strong positive association between the two rankings.

Watch out

  • The two regression lines (\(y\) on \(x\), and \(x\) on \(y\)) are different — use the right one for the prediction asked.
  • \(r=b_{yx}\cdot b_{xy}\) under a square root; and \(r\) always lies in \([-1,1]\) — a value outside signals an arithmetic error.
  • Correlation is not causation — a strong \(r\) doesn't prove one variable drives the other.
Exam focus: fitting a straight line by least squares and computing Karl Pearson's \(r\) from a table are both reliable questions.

How to work through this tutorial

Read actively. For each unit, cover the worked example and try it yourself first — then check. Passive reading of solved maths creates false confidence.

Build your own key-results sheet. Copy each "Key results" box by hand onto one page per unit. By exam time you revise from five sheets, not five chapters.

Then hit past papers. This tutorial teaches the method; Osmania University's previous question papers teach the exam. Alternate between them.

About this tutorial

A worked companion to the Engineering Mathematics I–IV syllabus for BE Biomedical Engineering, University College of Engineering (Autonomous), Osmania University. Concepts, key results and examples are standard treatments aligned to the prescribed texts (Grewal; Jain & Iyengar; Kreyszig). Equations render via MathJax.

Pair this with the syllabus & study-plan page. Worked examples are illustrative — always practise the full range from your prescribed textbooks and past Osmania University question papers.

Self-contained single file — upload index.html to tiiny.host to publish. Needs an internet connection only to load web fonts and the MathJax equation renderer.  Compiled study reference, not an official university publication.  Prepared 04 July 2026.
